info: The Holocene begins: birch woodland raced northward at over a kilometre per year, while the megafauna that had shaped the landscape for millennia were now functionally gone.
background: The Younger Dryas ended as abruptly as it began. Temperatures rose rapidly, and birch woodland re-expanded northward at remarkable speed — pollen data suggests spreading rates of over 1000 metres per year for pioneer species. Open birch woodland became the dominant landscape across much of the map area within a few centuries. Pine followed closely behind from eastern source populations. The herb layer remained relatively rich and open — hazel was beginning its expansion but not yet dominant. This is the opening of the Holocene proper, and a critical ecological threshold: the large herbivore community that had structured vegetation for millennia was now functionally gone across most of the map area. Fire set by Mesolithic hunter-gatherers was becoming the primary driver of landscape openness, replacing herbivory. Roughly 50–60% of the landscape was still fully open, with open woodland expanding rapidly.
